content.body: An ecosystem is all the living organisms (biotic) in an area plus their non-living environment (abiotic factors). Energy flows through ecosystems via food chains and webs.

Key terms:
• Producer: photosynthetic organism (plant, algae)
• Consumer: organism that eats other organisms
• Herbivore: eats plants (primary consumer)
• Carnivore: eats animals (secondary/tertiary consumer)
• Omnivore: eats both plants and animals
• Decomposer: breaks down dead organic matter
• Detritivore: feeds on dead organic matter

content.title: Food Web
content.body:
In a savanna:
Grass → Antelope → Lion
Grass → Zebra → Lion → Hyena
Grass → Grasshopper → Bird → Hawk

Food web = many interconnected food chains
More connections = more stable ecosystem
Removing a species affects all connected organisms

Energy transfer:
~10% of energy passes to next trophic level
90% lost as heat, movement, waste

Biological Magnification (bioaccumulation):
Toxins concentrate in higher trophic levels
Pesticide concentrations: plants < herbivores < carnivores < top predators

questions.question: A producer in an ecosystem is
options: an organism that makes its own food by photosynthesis
questions.answer: an organism that makes its own food by photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Producers (plants, algae, phytoplankton) convert light energy into chemical energy by photosynthesis.

questions.question: Which organism is a primary consumer?
questions.answer: Antelope eating grass
questions.explanation: Primary consumers (herbivores) eat producers directly. The antelope eats grass (producer).

questions.question: Decomposers are important because
options: they release nutrients back into soil from dead matter
questions.answer: they release nutrients back into soil from dead matter
questions.explanation: Bacteria and fungi decompose dead organisms, breaking down complex molecules and returning minerals to the soil.

questions.question: Why is only ~10% of energy transferred between trophic levels?
options: 90% is lost as heat, movement, respiration and waste
questions.answer: 90% is lost as heat, movement, respiration and waste
questions.explanation: At each level, organisms use most energy for their own respiration, movement, and warmth. Only ~10% is stored in biomass.

questions.question: Biological magnification means
options: toxin concentrations increase at higher trophic levels
questions.answer: toxin concentrations increase at higher trophic levels
questions.explanation: Toxins that aren't broken down accumulate in fat. Each predator eats many prey, so toxins concentrate (e.g. DDT in eagles).

questions.question: A food web is more stable than a single food chain because
options: alternative food sources exist if one species declines
questions.answer: alternative food sources exist if one species declines
questions.explanation: Multiple interconnections mean that if one prey species declines, predators can eat alternative prey.

questions.question: Removing a keystone predator from an ecosystem typically
options: causes prey to overpopulate and damage ecosystem
questions.answer: causes prey to overpopulate and damage ecosystem
questions.explanation: Keystone predators control prey populations. Without them, prey overpopulate, overeating plants and crashing the ecosystem.

questions.question: An omnivore is
options: an animal that only eats plants
an animal that eats both plants and animals
questions.answer: an animal that eats both plants and animals
questions.explanation: Omnivores eat from multiple trophic levels. Examples: humans, pigs, bears.

questions.question: Which trophic level has most energy available?
questions.answer: Producers
questions.explanation: Producers capture solar energy — they have most total energy. Each level above loses ~90%.

questions.question: Abiotic factors in an ecosystem include
options: temperature, rainfall, light, pH, soil type
questions.answer: temperature, rainfall, light, pH, soil type
questions.explanation: Abiotic = non-living. Physical/chemical factors: temperature, light, water, pH, minerals, wind, etc.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Matter (unlike energy) is recycled through ecosystems in biogeochemical cycles. The most important are the carbon cycle and nitrogen cycle.

Carbon cycle — carbon moves through:
• Photosynthesis: CO₂ → organic molecules (plants)
• Respiration: organic molecules → CO₂ (all organisms)
• Decomposition: dead matter → CO₂
• Combustion: fossil fuels → CO₂
• Ocean absorption: CO₂ dissolved in seawater
• Fossilisation: organic matter → coal/oil (over millions of years)

Nitrogen cycle:
• Nitrogen fixation: N₂ → NH₃ (Rhizobium bacteria in legume root nodules; lightning)
• Nitrification: NH₃ → NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻ (nitrifying bacteria)
• Assimilation: NO₃⁻ absorbed by plant roots → amino acids/proteins
• Ammonification: decomposers break proteins → NH₃
• Denitrification: NO₃⁻ → N₂ (denitrifying bacteria in waterlogged soil)

content.title: Carbon Cycle Disruption
content.body:
Human activities adding CO₂:
• Burning fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas)
• Deforestation (removes CO₂ absorbers)
• Cement production

Consequences: enhanced greenhouse effect → global warming → climate change

questions.question: Photosynthesis in the carbon cycle removes
options: CO₂ from atmosphere and fixes it into organic molecules
questions.answer: CO₂ from atmosphere and fixes it into organic molecules
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ and use it with water to synthesise glucose — removing carbon from the atmosphere.

questions.question: Which process returns carbon to the atmosphere?
questions.answer: Respiration and decomposition
questions.explanation: Respiration by all organisms and decomposition by bacteria/fungi release CO₂ back to atmosphere.

questions.question: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria are found in
questions.answer: legume root nodules
questions.explanation: Rhizobium bacteria live in nodules on legume roots (beans, peas, clover) and fix atmospheric N₂ to ammonia.

questions.question: Nitrification converts
questions.answer: ammonia to nitrates
questions.explanation: Nitrifying bacteria (Nitrosomonas, Nitrobacter) convert ammonia → nitrite → nitrate in the soil.

questions.question: Denitrification converts
questions.answer: nitrates back to N₂ gas
questions.explanation: Denitrifying bacteria in waterlogged/anaerobic soil convert nitrates → N₂, returning nitrogen to atmosphere.

questions.question: Deforestation increases atmospheric CO₂ because
options: fewer trees to absorb CO₂ by photosynthesis
questions.answer: fewer trees to absorb CO₂ by photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Removing trees reduces the amount of CO₂ being removed from the atmosphere by photosynthesis.

questions.question: Ammonification is carried out by
options: decomposers breaking down proteins in dead matter
questions.answer: decomposers breaking down proteins in dead matter
questions.explanation: Decomposers (bacteria, fungi) break down proteins and amino acids in dead organisms, releasing ammonia (NH₃).

questions.question: Plants absorb nitrogen from soil as
questions.answer: nitrate ions (NO₃⁻)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb inorganic nitrogen as nitrate ions through roots, then convert them to amino acids and proteins.

questions.question: Fossil fuels formed from
options: decomposed living organisms over millions of years
questions.answer: decomposed living organisms over millions of years
questions.explanation: Coal, oil, and gas formed from ancient plants and animals buried and compressed over millions of years — stored carbon.

questions.question: Lightning contributes to the nitrogen cycle by
options: fixing atmospheric N₂ to form nitrogen oxides
questions.answer: fixing atmospheric N₂ to form nitrogen oxides
questions.explanation: Lightning provides energy to fix N₂ from air into nitrogen oxides that dissolve in rain and reach soil as nitrates.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Human activities have significantly altered natural ecosystems. Key environmental problems include pollution, deforestation, overfishing, and climate change.

content.title: Pollution Types
content.body:
Water pollution:
• Eutrophication: fertiliser runoff → algal bloom → algae dies → decomposers use O₂ → fish die
• Sewage: pathogens, low oxygen
• Oil spills: smother marine organisms
• Plastic: ingested by wildlife

Air pollution:
• SO₂, NO₂ from combustion → acid rain
• CO₂, CH₄ → greenhouse effect
• CFCs → ozone layer depletion
• Particulates → respiratory disease

content.title: Deforestation Consequences
content.body:
• Loss of biodiversity (habitat destruction)
• Soil erosion (roots hold soil)
• Disruption of water cycle (trees release water vapour)
• Loss of carbon sink → increased CO₂
• Desertification in some regions
• Local climate change

Greenhouse Effect:
Sunlight → warms Earth → Earth emits infrared
Greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, H₂O, N₂O) absorb infrared → warm atmosphere

Enhanced greenhouse effect (human-caused):
→ Rising global temperatures
→ Ice melting → sea level rise
→ Extreme weather events
→ Species extinction
→ Crop failures

questions.question: Eutrophication begins when
options: fertiliser runs into water causing algal bloom
questions.answer: fertiliser runs into water causing algal bloom
questions.explanation: Nitrate/phosphate fertiliser washes into water → algae multiply rapidly (algal bloom) → water turns green.

questions.question: After an algal bloom, fish die because
options: decomposers breaking down dead algae use up all dissolved oxygen
questions.answer: decomposers breaking down dead algae use up all dissolved oxygen
questions.explanation: Dead algae are decomposed by bacteria that consume vast amounts of dissolved O₂, creating hypoxia that kills fish.

questions.question: Acid rain is caused by
options: SO₂ and NO₂ dissolving in rainwater
questions.answer: SO₂ and NO₂ dissolving in rainwater
questions.explanation: Burning fossil fuels releases SO₂ and NO₂ which dissolve in water to form sulphuric and nitric acid — acid rain.

questions.question: CFCs are harmful because
questions.answer: they deplete the ozone layer
questions.explanation: Chlorofluorocarbons (from old refrigerants/aerosols) break down ozone (O₃) in the stratosphere, allowing harmful UV through.

questions.question: Deforestation increases soil erosion because
options: tree roots no longer hold soil together
questions.answer: tree roots no longer hold soil together
questions.explanation: Tree roots bind soil particles. Without them, rain washes away topsoil, reducing fertility and causing landslides.

questions.question: Greenhouse gases trap heat by
options: absorbing infrared radiation emitted by Earth
questions.answer: absorbing infrared radiation emitted by Earth
questions.explanation: Greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, water vapour) absorb long-wave infrared radiation re-emitted by Earth's surface, warming the atmosphere.

questions.question: Biodiversity loss from habitat destruction is serious because
options: species provide ecosystem services and genetic resources
questions.answer: species provide ecosystem services and genetic resources
questions.explanation: Biodiversity provides food, medicines, pollination, pest control, etc. Lost species cannot be recovered.

questions.question: Sustainable development means
options: using resources as fast as possible
meeting current needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs
questions.answer: meeting current needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs
questions.explanation: Sustainable development: use resources at a rate they can be replenished, protecting the environment for future generations.

questions.question: Rising sea levels from climate change are caused by
options: melting ice sheets and thermal expansion of oceans
questions.answer: melting ice sheets and thermal expansion of oceans
questions.explanation: Warmer temperatures melt glaciers and ice caps, adding water to oceans. Warmer water also expands (thermal expansion).

questions.question: Which farming practice helps prevent eutrophication?
options: Calculating exact fertiliser needed and applying at right time
questions.answer: Calculating exact fertiliser needed and applying at right time
questions.explanation: Precision farming — applying the right amount of fertiliser at the right time — reduces runoff into waterways.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight

questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)

options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body:
Food chains & webs:
Producer → Primary consumer → Secondary consumer → Tertiary consumer
Energy transfer only ~10% per level (10% rule)

Ecological pyramids:
• Numbers: counts of organisms
• Biomass: total dry mass at each level
• Energy: always pyramid shape (energy always lost)

Nutrient cycles recycle matter:
Carbon cycle: photosynthesis, respiration, combustion, decomposition
Nitrogen cycle: fixation → nitrification → assimilation → denitrification

content.title: Energy Loss
content.body:
Grass → Rabbit → Fox → Eagle
10,000J → 1,000J → 100J → 10J
~90% lost at each level as heat (respiration), movement, reproduction, excretion, undigested material

Pyramid of biomass:
Oak tree(1000kg) → Caterpillars(10kg) → Blue tits(1kg)

content.title: Nitrogen Cycle
content.body:
N₂ in air → ammonia by nitrogen-fixing bacteria (Rhizobium in legume roots)
Ammonia → nitrites → nitrates by nitrifying bacteria
Nitrates absorbed by plants → amino acids → proteins
Decomposers break down dead matter → ammonia
Denitrifying bacteria: nitrates → N₂ (returns to atmosphere)

questions.question: Energy transfer between trophic levels is approximately
questions.explanation: 10% rule: ~90% of energy lost as heat, movement, waste. Only ~10% enters next trophic level

questions.question: Producers in food chains are
questions.answer: plants/algae that photosynthesise
questions.explanation: Producers: autotrophs making organic matter from CO₂ via photosynthesis. Base of all food chains

questions.question: Pyramid of energy always has true pyramid shape. Why?
options: energy lost at each trophic level → each level has less energy
questions.answer: energy lost at each trophic level → each level has less energy
questions.explanation: Energy pyramid always correct shape — energy never increases up chain. Numbers/biomass pyramids can be inverted

questions.question: Rhizobium bacteria are important because
options: they fix atmospheric N₂ into ammonia in legume root nodules
questions.answer: they fix atmospheric N₂ into ammonia in legume root nodules
questions.explanation: Nitrogen fixation: Rhizobium converts N₂ to NH₃ in root nodules of beans, peas, clover — natural fertiliser

questions.question: Denitrifying bacteria
options: convert nitrates back to N₂ gas
questions.answer: convert nitrates back to N₂ gas
questions.explanation: Denitrification: nitrates→N₂. Reduces soil fertility. More common in waterlogged anaerobic soils

questions.question: Carbon returns to atmosphere from living things by
questions.answer: respiration (all organisms) and decomposition
questions.explanation: All organisms release CO₂ via cellular respiration. Decomposers break down dead organic matter releasing CO₂

questions.question: Decomposers are ecologically important because
options: they recycle nutrients from dead organic matter back into the soil
questions.answer: they recycle nutrients from dead organic matter back into the soil
questions.explanation: Without decomposers, nutrients would be locked in dead matter — nutrient cycles would stop

questions.question: Bioaccumulation occurs when
options: fat-soluble toxins concentrate up the food chain
questions.answer: fat-soluble toxins concentrate up the food chain
questions.explanation: Top predators accumulate highest toxin concentrations (DDT, mercury) — biomagnification

questions.question: Deforestation affects the carbon cycle by
options: releasing stored CO₂ (burning) and removing CO₂ sinks
questions.answer: releasing stored CO₂ (burning) and removing CO₂ sinks
questions.explanation: Double impact: burning releases C; dead trees release C; fewer trees to absorb CO₂ → net increase in atmospheric CO₂

questions.question: Leguminous crops (beans, peas) improve soil fertility because
options: they form symbiosis with nitrogen-fixing Rhizobium
questions.answer: they form symbiosis with nitrogen-fixing Rhizobium
questions.explanation: Crop rotation using legumes: root nodules add fixed nitrogen to soil, reducing need for artificial fertilisers

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)

questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Ecology. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Ecology concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Ecology builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Ecology. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Ecology. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Ecology include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review
You have now covered all lessons in Ecology. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Ecology. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Ecology concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Ecology builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Ecology. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Ecology. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Ecology include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review
You have now covered all lessons in Ecology. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
